hypothesis: Incorporating a Dirichlet-process-based noise schedule into the forward diffusion process—replacing the standard Gaussian noise with Dirichlet-distributed perturbations that inherently respect the simplex geometry of compositional data—will simultaneously eliminate the need for post-hoc regularization penalties and improve density and coverage metrics beyond those achieved by PCDiff's additive constraint approach, because the generative trajectory would remain on the compositional manifold at every diffusion timestep rather than only being penalized for leaving it.
